html:
SCIA Thesis

SCIA is no longer a $5 sleepy OTC supplier, but the stock is still priced like the market does not trust the inflection. Last close was about $7.67 on May 15, 2026, or roughly $34 million of equity value. Against that, SCI had $8.54 million of cash and $3.37 million of marketable securities at March 31, 2026, no conventional debt, and about $1.0 million of operating lease obligations. The operating business is therefore valued around $23 million.

The clean thesis is not that SCI is secretly a silicon carbide ingot-feedstock supplier. The filings do not support that. The better thesis is narrower and more investable: SCIA is a tiny, qualified, domestic supplier of custom PVD thin-film materials, sputtering targets, ceramic targets, powders, bonding, recycling, repressing, and toll-processing services. Its products sit in unglamorous but critical production layers for glass, optical coatings, photonics, defense, solar, displays, and semiconductor-related applications. The company says its high-purity ceramic and oxide sputtering targets are used across semiconductors, thin-film solar, large-area glass coating, and photonics, including ITO, AZO, high-k dielectric, optical, and specialty coatings. That is the right part of the value chain for a micro-cap: qualification matters, order sizes can be too customized for larger suppliers, and once a process works, customers tend to reorder.

The market sees an OTC stock that just ran hard after Q1, screens at a mid-teens trailing multiple, has no analyst coverage, and has ugly customer concentration. That perception is not wrong, but it is stale. The May 2026 update showed a real break in the numbers: Q1 revenue rose 133% to a record $8.16 million, gross profit rose 90% to $2.04 million, and backlog reached $7.1 million versus $2.6 million at year-end and $2.5 million a year earlier. The company explicitly tied the backlog increase to products introduced in 2025 and new customers. That is the key line in the whole thesis.

GAAP Q1 net income was only $462,000, or $0.10 per diluted share, but that includes a $562,000 fraud expense from the February imposter scam. Add that back after tax and Q1 earnings power was closer to $0.20 per share. Annualizing one quarter is dangerous in this business, but the backlog and customer deposits make it less reckless than usual. Customer deposits jumped to $3.56 million from $829,000 at year-end, and work-in-process inventory expanded sharply. That looks more like orders being staged than a one-quarter miracle.

The asset base is also underappreciated. This is not a story requiring a dilutive factory build. Property and equipment at cost is $10.9 million, net PP&E is $3.0 million, and the company already has vacuum hot pressing, indium bonding, powder processing, ceramic target, and reclaim capabilities. Its services page describes the business as a bridge from R&D to full-scale production, which is exactly where a tiny specialist can win. The 2025 10-K says SCI introduced an innovative rotatable precious metal target product for photonics and an ITO product in Q2 2025, while also pushing reclaim, repress, vacuum hot press, cold isostatic press, and kiln utilization. The Q1 numbers are the first evidence that these initiatives may be converting into orders.

Valuation is still cheap if Q1 is an inflection rather than a spike. TTM revenue is now about $24.3 million. TTM GAAP net income is roughly $1.9 million, but adjusted for the fraud charge, it is closer to $2.3 million. At $7.67, SCIA is about 18x GAAP TTM earnings, but only about 15x adjusted TTM earnings and roughly 10x adjusted earnings after net cash. On a forward view, a conservative $28 million to $32 million revenue year at mid-20s gross margin and no repeat fraud charge can produce $0.60 to $0.80 of EPS. Ex net cash of about $2.45 per share, the stock is trading near 7x to 9x that current-cycle operating earnings power.

The torque is in operating leverage, not financial leverage. Q1 gross margin was only 24.9%, down from 30.6% a year ago because of raw material costs and mix. If new products, rotatable targets, ITO, enriched boron carbide, reclaim, and toll services improve mix while the fixed cost base stays controlled, the next $5 million to $10 million of annual revenue can matter disproportionately. A $4 million EBIT business at 10x plus net cash is an $11 stock. A $6 million to $7 million EBIT business at a still-modest specialty-materials multiple is a $18 to $22 stock.

Capital allocation has changed too. SCI authorized a $1 million buyback in November 2025, bought back $500,000 in December, and bought another $267,500 in Q1. At March 31, treasury stock was 150,000 shares at $767,500 of cost. The buyback is small in dollars, but not small relative to float. StockAnalysis lists insider ownership around 40%, institutional ownership under 4%, and a float around 2.7 million shares. That setup creates both risk and upside: exits are hard, but small incremental demand can move the stock violently, as shown by the 41% move on Q1 results.

The bear case is simple and serious. The 2025 10-K says the top two customers were 84.5% of sales, with the largest at 66%, and there are no minimum purchase obligations. If the largest customer cuts orders, this can go back to being a lumpy, undercovered micro-cap with excess cash and uneven margins. The February bank fraud also matters. The business was not impaired, and some funds were recovered, but it is a governance and controls blemish. Still, the balance sheet gives time: cash plus securities cover roughly one-third of the market cap, and the company is producing cash rather than asking for it.

The best variant perception is that SCIA is not a semiconductor hype stock. It is a cash-rich, domestic, custom thin-film materials supplier that just proved demand for new products and new customers is real. The stock has already moved from ignored to noticed, but it is not yet priced as an earnings inflection. The next two quarters matter. If backlog converts, deposits turn into revenue, and the fraud charge disappears, the market will have to stop valuing SCIA on stale 2025 revenue decline optics.

Bear target: $4.75, about 38% downside from $7.67. This assumes revenue slips back toward $22 million, gross margin stays in the low-20s, and the market values the operating business at only 6x to 7x depressed EBIT plus net cash.

Base target: $11.50, about 50% upside. This assumes 2026 revenue around $30 million, gross margin around 26% to 27%, EBIT around $3.5 million to $4.0 million, and a 9x to 10x EV/EBIT multiple plus net cash.

Bull target: $20.00, about 161% upside. This assumes new products and customers keep scaling, revenue reaches $38 million to $42 million, gross margin approaches 29% to 30%, EBIT reaches $6 million to $7 million, and the market pays 11x to 12x EV/EBIT for a debt-free specialty materials supplier with visible growth.
